title: Multifrequency Floquet Engineering of Magnon Polaritons
authors: L. Hackner, A. R. Myatt, W. Wustmann, N. J. Lambert
abstract: Floquet engineering of cavity magnon-polaritons by periodically modulating the magnon frequency has recently attracted much interest as a way to manipulate the energy spectrum of magnon-photon hybrid systems. However, modulating the frequency of magnons by a time-varying bias magnetic field can be challenging. We demonstrate cavity magnon-polariton Floquet engineering by modulating the microwave cavity frequency, allowing large modulation depth and bandwidth. We apply commensurate two-frequency Floquet modulations with the higher frequency at twice and three times the lower frequency, and demonstrate that the resulting spectrum depends on the relative amplitude and phase of the two drive tones. In comparison with single-frequency Floquet modulations, the spectrum has qualitatively different features; in particular, new anticrossings appear between previously uncoupled sidebands. Our platform offers an alternative way to manipulate Floquet quasi-energy levels in hybrid systems.
summary: The paper presents a method for Floquet engineering of magnon-polaritons by modulating the microwave cavity frequency instead of the magnetic field. This approach enables larger modulation depths and allows for complex spectrum manipulation through multi-frequency drives. The study reveals new features like additional anticrossings and the formation of dark modes.
main_problem: Traditional Floquet engineering of magnon-polaritons via magnetic field modulation is experimentally difficult due to the requirement for large-amplitude, rapid frequency tuning. The authors seek an alternative method using cavity frequency modulation to achieve larger modulation depths and bandwidths.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that modulating the microwave cavity frequency allows for effective Floquet engineering, including the creation of new anticrossings between previously uncoupled sidebands using multi-frequency commensurate drives. They also observe the emergence of 'dark' modes due to destructive interference between sidebands.
method: The researchers use an arbitrary waveform generator and an IQ demodulator to apply sinusoidal frequency modulation to a microwave cavity frequency. The transmission spectrum is measured using a vector network analyzer (VNA) and analyzed using Floquet matrix formalism and cavity input-output theory.
model_or_system: A hybrid cavity electromagnonic system consisting of a microwave resonator (coplanar waveguide loop) coupled to a Yttrium Iron Garnet (YIG) sphere in the strong coupling regime. The system is described by a time-periodic Hamiltonian involving a time-varying cavity frequency.
key_observables: Transmission coefficient (|S21|), sideband appearance, and anticrossing gaps (mode splitting).
important_parameters: Coupling rate (g/2pi approx 20.7 MHz), cavity/magnon frequencies (approx 2.17 GHz), modulation frequency (5 to 180 MHz), and modulation amplitude (10 to 50 MHz).
main_assumptions: The Floquet matrix is truncated at sideband orders n = +/- 8; the system is modeled using cavity input-output theory.
figures_summary: The figures likely show the transmission spectrum (S21) under single-frequency modulation, the appearance of sidebands, and the complex spectrum resulting from multi-frequency (m=2, 3) modulation including new anticrossings.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of magnetic modulation, describes the experimental setup and cavity modulation technique, presents results for single-tone Floquet engineering, extends the study to multi-frequency commensurate drives, and concludes with the implications for hybrid system manipulation.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates a new way to control quasi-energy levels and sideband dynamics in hybrid systems using multi-tone driving, specifically highlighting interference-induced dark modes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental setup and device characterisa- tion. (a) Schematic the hybrid magnonic device compris- ing a microwave ring resonator with gain (Amp) and phase control (IQ), and an embedded YIG sphere (right). A vec- tor network analyser (VNA) measures the transmission (S21) of a feedline, and an arbitrary waveform generator (AWG) allows the frequency of the cavity modes to be modulated. (b) |S21| of uncoupled cavity mode spectrum under single tone frequency modulation as function of drive frequency Ω/2π, with ωc/2π = 2.615 GHz, showing sidebands with min- ima coincident with Bessel function zeros (top axis, dotted lines.) (c) |S21| as a function of applied magnetic field, with ωc/2π =...

FIG. 2. |S21| of feedline for two-frequency cavity modulation with Ω2 = 2Ω1, a1/2π = 40 MHz, and ϕ = 0. Ω1/2π is stepped from 5 MHz to 60 MHz. Rows from top to bottom correspond to a2/2π = 10 MHz, a2/2π = 25 MHz, a2/2π = 40 MHz. Left column shows experimental data; right column – input-output theory (see main text).

FIG. 3. |S21| of feedline for two-frequency cavity modulation with Ω2 = 2Ω1, a1/2π = 35 MHz, and a2/2π = 40 MHz. Ω1/2π is stepped from 5 MHz to 60 MHz. Rows from top to bottom correspond to ϕ = 0, ϕ = π/2, ϕ = π, ϕ = 3π/2. Left column shows experimental data; right column – input- output theory.

FIG. 4. |S21| of feedline for two-frequency cavity modulation with Ω2 = 3Ω1, a1/2π = 32.5 MHz, and ϕ = 0. Ω1/2π is stepped from 5 MHz to 60 MHz. Rows from top to bottom correspond to a2/2π = 20 MHz, a2/2π = 30 MHz, a2/2π = 40 MHz. Left column shows experimental data; right column – input-output theory.

Spintronics.note: The paper directly addresses the physics of magnon-polaritons in a hybrid cavity-magnon system, specifically using Floquet engineering to manipulate the spectrum of coupled spin-photon modes.
